revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Shinto shrine]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/24756154998|commit triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
[[commons:Category:Izusahime-jinja]][[ja:伊豆佐比売神社]] {{Expand Japanese|伊豆佐比売神社}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Izusahime Shrine | native_name = 伊豆佐比売神社 | image = Izusahime jinja (Spring festival).JPG | mapframe = yes | mapframe-zoom = 15 | mapframe-wikidata = yes | mapframe-point = none | map_type = Japan | map_caption = Izusahime Shrine | coordinates = {{coord|38.322227|140.9638}} | map_relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] }} {{nihongo|'''Izusahime Shrine'''|伊豆佐比売神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Mutsu Province]]. == instance of (P31) == * {{ill|Shinto shrine|lt=Shinto shrine|ar|جينجا|arz|ضريح شنتو|bcl|Shinto shrine|ca|Jinja|cs|Šintoistická svatyně|da|Shinto-tempel|de|Shintō-Schrein|el|Σιντοϊστικό Ιερό|en|Shinto shrine|eo|Ŝintoa sanktejo|es|Jinja|eu|Jinja|fa|معبد شینتویی|fi|Šintolainen pyhäkkö|fr|Sanctuaire shinto|hi|शिंतो मंदिर|hr|Šintoistički hram|hu|Sintó szentély|id|Jinja|it|Jinja|ja|神社|ka|ძინძია|ko|신사 (신토)|lb|Shintō-Schräin|mr|शिंतो मंदिर|nl|Jinja (heiligdom)|no|Jinja|or|ଜିଞ୍ଜା|pl|Jinja|pt|Templo xintoísta|ru|Дзиндзя|sc|Jinja|simple|Shinto shrine|sl|Šintoistično svetišče|sr|Шинтоистички храм|sv|Jinja|th|ศาลเจ้าชินโต|tr|Şinto tapınağı|uk|Святилище (синто)|vi|Thần xã|wuu|神社|zh_yue|神社|zh|神社|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} * {{ill|Shikinaisha|lt=Shikinaisha|en|Shikinaisha|zh|式內社|qid=Q134917286|1=Shikinaisha}} * {{ill|Shikinai Shosha|lt=Shikinai Shōsha|en|Shikinai Shosha|ja|式内小社|simple|Shikinai Shosha|qid=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}} * {{ill|Q135160342|ltq=Q135160342|qid=Q135160342|1=Kokuhei-sha}} * {{ill|Q135026601|ltq=Q135026601|qid=Q135026601|1=Shikinai Hiteisha}} == part of (P361) == * {{ill|Q11658590|ltq=Q11658590|qid=Q11658590|1=List of Shikinaisha in Mutsu Province}} ** Series ordinal: 11 ** P155: {{ill|Sakueno Shrine|lt=Sakueno Shrine|qid=Q135039985|1=Sakueno Shrine}} ** P156: {{ill|Q133753|ltq=Q133753|qid=Q133753|1=Shiogama shrine}} ** P156: {{ill|Q135091221|ltq=Q135091221|qid=Q135091221|1=Shihahikono Shrine}} == located in the administrative territorial entity (P131) == * {{ill|Rifu|lt=Rifu|ar|ريفو (مايغي)|arz|ريفو|ceb|Rifu Chō|cs|Rifu|de|Rifu|en|Rifu|es|Rifu (Miyagi)|eu|Rifu|fa|ریفو|fr|Rifu|id|Rifu, Miyagi|it|Rifu|ja|利府町|ko|리후정|lt|Rifu|mg|Rifu|mr|रिफु (मियागी)|ms|Rifu, Miyagi|my|ရိဖုမြို့|nl|Rifu|pl|Rifu|pt|Rifu|ru|Рифу|sv|Rifu|tl|Rifu, Miyagi|tr|Rifu|uk|Ріфу|ur|ریفو|zh_yue|利府町|zh|利府町|qid=Q1202986|1=Rifu}} * {{ill|Q907389|ltq=Q907389|qid=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}} * {{ill|Miyagi District|lt=Miyagi district|bcl|Distrito nin Miyagi, Miyagi|ceb|Miyagi Gun|de|Miyagi-gun|en|Miyagi District|fr|District de Miyagi|id|Distrik Miyagi, Miyagi|it|Distretto di Miyagi|ja|宮城郡|ko|미야기군|pl|Powiat Miyagi|ru|Мияги (уезд)|uk|Повіт Міяґі|zh_yue|宮城郡|zh|宮城郡|qid=Q1131751|1=Miyagi District}} == coordinate location (P625) == * {{coord|38.322227|140.9638}} == image (P18) == * Izusahime jinja (Spring festival).JPG == Kokugakuin University Digital Museum entry ID (P13677) == * 182048 ** P3831: {{ill|Kokugakuin University Shrine database|lt=Kokugakuin University Shrine database|qid=Q135159299|1=Kokugakuin University Shrine database}} == modern shrine ranking (P13723) == * {{ill|Q134917284|ltq=Q134917284|qid=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} ** End time: {'time': '+1946-02-02T00:00:00Z', 'timezone': 0, 'before': 0, 'after': 0, 'precision': 11, 'calendarmodel': 'http://www.wikidata.org/entity/Q1985727'} ** P1534: {{ill|Q3029647|ltq=Q3029647|qid=Q3029647|1=Shinto Directive}} == P14005 (P14005) == * {{ill|Senior Fifth Rank|lt=Senior Fifth Rank|ja|正五位|ko|정5위|zh|正五位|qid=Q11123280|1=Senior Fifth Rank}} == country (P17) == * ᥓᥣᥱ ᥙᥣᥢᥱ|tet|Japaun|te|జపాన్|tg|Ҷопон|th|ประเทศญี่ปุ่น|thnews|หมวดหมู่:ประเทศญี่ปุ่น|tig|ጃፓን|ti|ጃፓን|tk|Ýaponiýa|tl|Hapon|tly|Japonijə|tn|Japan|tok|ma Nijon|to|Siapani|tpi|Siapan|trv|Tanah Tunux|tr|Japonya|trnews|Kategori:Japonya|trquote|Japonya|trvoyage|Japonya|ts|Japani|tt|Япония|tum|Japan|tw|Japan|tyv|Япония|ty|Tāpōnē|udm|Япония|ug|ياپونىيە|uk|Японія|uknews|Категорія:Японія|ukquote|Японія|ukvoyage|Японія|ur|جاپان|uz|Yaponiya|vec|Japon|vep|Japonii|ve|Japan|vi|Nhật Bản|vibooks|Các quốc gia/Nhật Bản|vivoyage|Nhật Bản|vls|Japan|vo|Yapän|war|Hapon|wa|Djapon|wo|Sapoŋ|wuu|日本|xal|Ниxуудин Нутг|xh|IJapani|xmf|იაპონია|yi|יאפאן|yo|Japan|za|Nyiedbonj|zea|Japan|zgh|ⵏⵉⴱⵓⵏ|zh_classical|日本|zh_min_nan|Ji̍t-pún|zh_yue|日本|zh|日本|zhnews|Category:日本|zhquote|日本|zhvoyage|日本|zu|IJaphani|qid=Q17|1=Japan}} == name in kana (P1814) == * いずさひめじんじゃ == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 5370605000287 == Commons category (P373) == * Izusahime-jinja == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '宮城県宮城郡利府町菅谷飯土井字長者55', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Izusahime-jinja}} [[JA:伊豆佐比売神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11380543}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Izu Sahime Shrine |Image = [[File:Izusahime jinja (Spring festival).JPG|270px|Izusahime Shrine Worship Hall]]
Worship Hall |Location = {{ill|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|Q47896|qid=Q47896|1=Miyagi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Miyagi District|Q1131751|qid=Q1131751|1=Miyagi District}}{{ill|lt=Rifu Town|Q1202986|qid=Q1202986|1=Rifu}}Sugaya Idoi Aza Choja 55 |Position = {{Coord|38|19|20.45|N|140|57|49.45|E|scale:10000_region:jp-04_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = Izu Sahibi Mikoto |Company rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}} (small), former {{ill|lt=murasha|Q134917284|qid=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |Foundation = Legend・{{ill|lt=Emperor Tenmu|Q335181|qid=Q335181|1=Emperor Tenmu}}2nd year ({{ill|lt=673rd|Q31105|qid=Q31105|1=673}}) |Main shrine = {{ill|lt=Ryuzo|Q210684|qid=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |Alias = Mihime no Miya |Regular festival = April 15th, September 15th |Shinto ritual = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Miyagi|label=Izu Sahibi
Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Izusahime jinja Gate.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Izusahime Shrine Torii]] [[File:Izusahime jinja (inside).JPG|250px|right|thumb|Inside the worship hall of Izusahime Shrine
Beyond the blinds, the main hall is a vermilion-painted shrine with a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sakaki|1=UNKNOWN}} offering. The main shrine is completely surrounded by walls and a canopy to protect it from rain and wind, so it cannot be seen from the outside.
Taken with permission during the annual festival. ]] [[File:Precincts of Izusahime jinja.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Ruins of the Kumon Choja Mansion
The top of the hill is a fairly wide flat area, and it is said that in the past there was the Kumon Choja mansion of a wealthy farmer. The precincts of Izu Sahime Shrine are part of a hill. ]] [[File:Iidoi inari myoujin.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Idoi Inari Myojin Shrine and old zelkova stocks
To the left of the Idoi Inari Myojin shrine, under the rain cover of the corrugated iron roof, there are old zelkova stocks and returned zelkova wood. ]]'''Izu Sahime Shrine'' (Izu Sahime Shrine) is a {{ill|lt=Shrine|Q845945|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|Q47896|qid=Q47896|1=Miyagi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Miyagi District|Q1131751|qid=Q1131751|1=Miyagi District}}{{ill|lt=Rifu Town|Q1202986|qid=Q1202986|1=Rifu}}. Shikinai-sha is listed as a small shrine in "{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}," and {{ill|lt=Modern shrine rank system|Q712534|qid=Q712534|lt=Old shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} is {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|qid=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. It is enshrined on a small hill called ``Chōja (Nine Gates) Mansion''. === Enshrined God === {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|qid=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} When I visited the {{ill|lt=chief priest|Q11453500|qid=Q11453500|1=Gūji}} in April 2021 ({{ill|lt=2009|Q1996|qid=Q1996|1=2009}}), I was told that the {{ill|lt=enshrined deity|Q11591100|qid=Q11591100|1=Saijin}} of this shrine is ``Izu Sahime no Mikoto''. It is said to be a god listed in the Engishiki Shinmyocho. Q1996}}) During the annual festival on April 15th, I heard from the representative of the shrine parishioners that it is also called ``Izu Samo''.) It is said to be enshrined as the god of good harvest. "Engishiki Nairiku Oku 100-za Heisei Pilgrimage Record"Kenma Honda "Engishiki Nairiku Oku 100-za Heisei Pilgrimage Record" Shrine Shimpo Project June 1997. states that it is unclear what kind of god Izu Sahime no Mikoto is, according to research in the ``Dainippon Shinto Name Dictionary'' Edited by Yoshihiko Umeda, ``Dainippon Shinto Name Dictionary'', Hori Shoten, December 1972, and offers two theories regarding the deity enshrined at this shrine. #``God of hot water'' theory:{{ill|lt=Enkei Deguchi|Q108780926|qid=Q108780926|1=Deguchi Nobutsune}}'s "{{ill|lt=Shinmeicho kosho|Q11588824|qid=Q11588824|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō Studies}}" volume 5"Shinmeicho kosho" is edited by Yoshi Saeki, "Jingi Zensho Volume 1", Koten Kokyukyo Included in October 1906. states, ``{{ill|lt=Izu Province|Q860928|qid=Q860928|1=Izu Province}}Izu Nabi Mikoto Shrine, Ankokurei, {{ill|lt=Dewa Province|Q876032|qid=Q876032|1=Dewa Province}}{{ill|lt=Yuzu Sami Shrine|Q11577650|qid=Q11577650|lt=Yuzume Samo Shrine|1=Yuzusame Shrine}}.'' Yuzusa of Yuzusamo Shrine means ``Yudezawa'' and is said to be the guardian deity of hot springsIn the Engishiki Nairiku 100-za Heisei Pilgrimage Record, the enshrined deity of Yuzusamo Shrine is Mizohime no Mikoto, who is also worshiped as the god of hot springs in various places. k|Ōkuninushi|Q276944|Omiki}} and {{ill|lt=Sukunabikona|Q3275687|qid=Q3275687|lt=Sukohikona|1=Sukunabikona}} are also enshrined, so it may not be possible to conclude that Mizohime no Mikoto is the god of hot springs alone. . Furthermore, from "Rifu Town History", edited by Rifu Town History Compilation Committee, "Rifu Town History", Rifu Town History Compilation Committee, March 1986. "Yumusahime Shrine in Tagawa District, Dewa Province (currently {{ill|lt=Yamagata Prefecture|Q125863|qid=Q125863|1=Yamagata Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Tsuruoka City|Q734416|qid=Q734416|1=Tsuruoka}} Yutagawa)" The same deity was enshrined, and the springing of hot springs was probably considered a divine act, and the goddess was the god who created things.I wonder if there was hot springs in Sawaoto or Unohara at that time. Today, there are two hot springs still flowing within 4km of this shrine, and one of these, ``Sawaoto-no-Yu,'' is located in the Enoki River basin, and the ``Rokuta Ruins'', the remains of early Jomon settlements, are located on the hills leading from the shrine to the Enoki River.{{ill|lt=Showa|Q327567|qid=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}}The excavation in 1985|Q2431}} seems to be unrelated to the development of this land and the construction of this shrine.#“God of grain spirits” theory :The enshrinement site ``Iidoi'' is said to be an accent of ``Iitoyo'' by Kaoru Hongo, author of ``Dewa Mutsu no Kosha'' in his book, and he makes the following inference. In other words, among the 100 {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|qid=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}} shrines listed in the ``Engishiki Shinmecho,'' the god where hot springs gush out is called the ``Onsen Shrine,'' and the shrines associated with ``Iitoyo'' are said to be ``the god of granaries'' and ``the god of production,'' rather than the ``god of hot springs.'' Also, ``Shinmeicho Kosho Volume 5'' I introduced this and considered that it would not be unnatural to enshrine the god of grain spirits, considering the topography of the enshrined place and the reason why the wealthy farmer {{ill|lt=#Kumonchoja Residence|UNKNOWN|lt=Kumonchoja}} was established.Also, in the "Engishiki Nairiku Oku 100 Za Heisei Pilgrimage Record" , "Ou Kanseimon Roshi" written in {{ill|lt=Kyoho|Q1145048|qid=Q1145048|1=Kyōhō}}4 ({{ill|lt=1719|Q6902|qid=Q6902|1=1719}}) Yoshikazu Sakuma From “Ou Kansekimon Roshi” edited by Hobundo Publishing, October 1972. mentions that the enshrined deity is Kausahime, whose furigana in ``Hunai Fudoki'' is ``Mizokuihime'', and the same book does not include any special description of Mizokui Hime, but only says ``Keida 28 bundles Sanke Tadokoro Festival Mizokui Himeya.'' Furthermore, from "Hunai Fudoki Volume 4" in the first year of {{ill|lt=Yasunaga (era name)|Q1193910|qid=Q1193910|lt=Yasunaga|1=An'ei}} ({{ill|lt=1772|Q7690|qid=Q7690|1=1772}}) "Hunai Fudoki" edited by Nozomi Tanabe, published by Hobundo Publishing, November 1975. , etc., have errors in the exact same part regarding the addition of this shrine. The article dated August 7, 2007 ({{ill|lt=852|Q30383|qid=Q30383|1=852}}) states that he is ``Kasho 5th rank, lower rank,'' but in ``Ou Kanseimon Roshi'' and ``Hunai Fudoki,'' it is written in July 2017 as ``Jishō 5th lower rank.'' From this, it is speculated that various magazines written afterward, such as ``Hunai Fudoki,'' were written based on ``Ou Kansekimon Roshi.''Based on these, “Engishiki Nairiku Oku 100 Za Heisei Pilgrimage Record”, edited by the Miyagi Prefectural Shrine Agency, which lists the enshrined deity as Mizokui Himen-no-Mikoto, "Miyagi Prefecture Shrine Directory", Miyagi Prefecture Shrine Agency, October 1976. has written at the end of the section for this shrine, ``The {{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmecho|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|lt=Shiki|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} says Izu Samo Shrine, and the {{ill|lt=Japanese Emperor Montoku Annals|Q1052905|qid=Q1052905|lt=Buntoku Jitsuroku|1=Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku}} is made for Izu Sama Shrine.How about making it the shrine transmission Mizokuihiba...''. From "Rifu Village Journal" Edited by Rifu Village Village Journal Editorial Committee, "Rifu Village History", Rifu Village Office, March 1963. says, ``This shrine is a female, primarily in charge of water irrigation in the rice-growing area, and the shrine has been revered day and night throughout the area since ancient times, since the paddy fields were cultivated.'' Based on what is written here, it is considered that the deity of this shrine is either Izu Sahime no Mikoto or {{ill|lt=Toyokebime|Q2983724|qid=Q2983724|lt=Toyoukehime no Mikoto|1=Toyouke-hime}}, who is the ``goddess of a bountiful harvest.''Another different theory is “Engishiki Nairiku Oku 100 Za Heisei Pilgrimage Record”In the 7th year of An'ei ({{ill|lt=1778|Q7713|qid=Q7713|1=1778}}), the ``Engishiki Mutsu 100 Za worship records'' states, ``Itsu Sahime no Mikoto-no-seki, the commoner Akutama Gozen toun.'' (Akutama Gozen For details, see {{ill|lt=#Kumonchoja-yashiki Ruins|UNKNOWN|lt=Kumonchoja-yashiki Ruins}} below), but it also states that it seems inappropriate to connect Akutama Gozen with the deity enshrined at this shrine. In any case, both the chief priest and parishioners currently enshrine the deity of this shrine as Izu Sahime no Mikoto, the god of bountiful harvests. === History ===The year of its establishment is unknown, but it is written in "Ou Kansekimon Roshi" Mitsunori Yasuda, "Shinsen Mutsu Fudoki", Rekishi Toshosha, November 1980. According to , in {{ill|lt=Emperor Tenmu|Q335181|qid=Q335181|1=Emperor Tenmu}}2 ({{ill|lt=private year|Q11248480|qid=Q11248480|1=unofficial era name}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hakuho|1=UNKNOWN}}2, {{ill|lt=673|Q31105|qid=Q31105|1=673}}), he is said to have held a divine festival by dedicating Keida (a rice field given by the god of heaven for rituals), and in ``Hunai Meisekishi'' it is further written as ``an ancient shrine.'' However, “Rifu Town History”At this time, {{ill|lt=Tagajo|Q870867|qid=Q870867|1=Taga Castle}} had not yet been established, and with the establishment of Tagajo, the four shrines in Miyagi District were enshrined. Kamado Shrine #Shibahiko Shrine|Q133753|Shibahiko Shrine}} (currently Shibahiko Shrine Shiogama Shrine), {{ill|lt=Hanabushi Shrine|Q11679017|qid=Q11679017|1=Hanabue Shrine}}, {{ill|lt=Taga Shrine (Tagajo City)|Q11431090|qid=Q11431090|lt=Taga Shrine|1=Taga Shrine (Tagajō)}} 4 shrines. Also does not exist around this time, and the remaining editions of ``{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sokoku Fudoki|1=UNKNOWN}}'', which is the basis for this theory, include the following: ``Izu Sami Shrine, Keida 28 Tsukasa Mikeda, Tokosai Mizosai Himanya, Emperor Tenmu's 2nd Year Ceremony Keida Gyojinrei, Arikami Family Shrine, etc.'' is a theory that cannot be accepted because it appears to be a forgery. Regarding this, the "Engishiki Nairiku Oku 100 Za Heisei Pilgrimage Record" states that at that time {{ill|lt=Ezo|Q932574|qid=Q932574|1=Emishi}} was subdued several times, and also that "Japanese Gods - Shrines and Sacred Sites - 12 Tohoku and Hokkaido" edited by Kenichi Tanigawa "Japanese Gods -Shrines and Sacred Sites- 12 Tohoku/Hokkaido” Hakusuisha Co., Ltd. June 1984. As argued by , there seems to have been shrines of gods that the local people believed in, and it is reasonable to deny the theory that Emperor Tenmu was born in 2 years."{{ill|lt=Nihon Montoku Emperor's Annals|Q1052905|qid=Q1052905|1=Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku}}" In the article dated August 7, {{ill|lt=Ninju|Q1373077|qid=Q1373077|1=Ninju}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=852 years|Q30383|qid=Q30383|1=852}}), this shrine There is a record that {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shōgoi Lower|1=UNKNOWN}} was conferred the {{ill|lt=Divine grade|Q11591025|qid=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}}, but since it says ``Kasho Fifth Grade Lower'', it is possible that the divine rank was conferred before that. In {{ill|lt=Extension (era name)|Q1327274|qid=Q1327274|lt=Extension|1=Enchō}} in 5 years ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|qid=Q30470|1=927}}), he was listed as a small shrine in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|lt=Engishiki system|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}.The subsequent history is unknown, but the shrine building dating from around the 12th to 13th century was destroyed by a fire in February of {{ill|lt=Manji|Q1145295|qid=Q1145295|1=Manji (era)}}3 ({{ill|lt=1660|Q7001|qid=Q7001|1=1660}}). (Japan)|Q1193848|Mashinori}} It is said that the shrine was built in 1715|Q6884}} by {{ill|lt=Sendai Domain|Q1062182|qid=Q1062182|1=Sendai Domain}}{{ill|lt=Date Tsunamura|Q5227557|qid=Q5227557|lt=4th lord Tsunamura|1=Date Tsunamura}}From the ``opinion regarding historical origins'' by Ichiemon Tanno, the mayor of Rifu village, dated July 29, 1926 ({{ill|lt=1926|Q5259|qid=Q5259|1=1926}}). . The size of the shrine built by Tsunamura, the fourth lord of the domain, is unknown, but the "Yasunaga Shode" written in {{ill|lt=Yasunaga (era name)|Q1193910|qid=Q1193910|lt=Yasunaga|1=An'ei}} 3rd year ({{ill|lt=1774|Q7697|qid=Q7697|1=1774}}) records the size of the shrine at that time as ``3 shakusaku of the shrine.'' At some point, it became the {{ill|lt=terminal shrine|Q135009906|qid=Q135009906|1=Massha}} of {{ill|lt=Shigama Shrine|Q133753|qid=Q133753|1=Shiogama shrine}} (currently Shibahiko Shrine/Shigama Shrine), but the circumstances behind this are not well understood. In the "Shinsen Mutsu Fudoki" , there is a question ``Is it possible that it is a subsidiary shrine of Shiogamasha?'', so it seems that it was considered a subsidiary shrine in the late {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|qid=Q184963|1=Edo period}} period.During the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|qid=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}}, the sacred precincts of this shrine became a camp, and buildings such as the shrine building were destroyed in battle, and the ceremony was temporarily absent.From the ``Shinto Statement Report'' dated 8th year of Meiji ({{ill|lt=1875|Q7764|qid=Q7764|1=1875}}). . {{ill|lt=Taisho|Q329174|qid=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 15th year ({{ill|lt=1926|Q5259|qid=Q5259|1=1926}}) “Opinion on historical origins” by the village chief of Rifu Miyagi County Educational Association From "Miyagi Gunshi", edited by Meishon Publishing, June 1972. states that on May 5, {{ill|lt=Showa|Q327567|qid=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=1927|Q18110|qid=Q18110|1=1927}}), the decision to raise the village to the status of a village shrine was made through the efforts of the local people.In December of the 5th year of the Taisho era ({{ill|lt=1916|Q2087|qid=Q2087|1=1916}}), the five shrines of Koto Shrine and Kumano Shrine in Sawaoto, Kumano Shrine in Kamiyasawa, Kumano Shrine in Sugaya, and Kamo Shrine in Irisugaya were enshrined together, and in the 9th year of the Taisho era ({{ill|lt=1920|Q2155|qid=Q2155|1=1920}}), the main shrine and worship hall were newly built. It is now independent from Shiogama Shrine. “Rifu Village Journal”Currently, it is enshrined at {{ill|lt=Sendai City|Q46747|qid=Q46747|1=Sendai}}{{ill|lt=Miyagino Ward|Q1043355|qid=Q1043355|1=Miyagino-ku, Sendai}} Iwakiri Aza Inari Nishi. ([https://www.google.co.jp/maps?ll=38.2985,140.933722&spn=0.01,0.01&q=38.2985,140.933722 See google map]). The existing torii gate was erected in November of {{ill|lt=Showa|Q327567|qid=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}}54 ({{ill|lt=1979|Q2484|qid=Q2484|1=1979}}) and is not facing our company. . === Company name === The name registered with the Miyagi Prefectural Shrine Agency is ``Izu Sahime Shrine.'' It is sometimes written as ``Izusahime Shrine'' or ``Izu Satsuhime Shrine,'' but the plaque inside the worship hall and the ``Engishiki Shinmyocho'' are written as ``Izusame Shrine.'' In "Engishiki Nairiku Oku 100 Za, Heisei Pilgrimage Record" , it is written in "Japanese Emperor Montoku Annals" as {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|qid=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}}'''Izusama-jin'', and this ``''''' is definitely ``''me''', so it is considered that ``Izusame Shrine'' is correct. Also, in the "Shinsen Mutsu Fudoki" , it is written that "local people call it '''the palace of the princess''', but in the "Engishiki Nairiku Oku 100 Theaters Heisei Pilgrimage Record" '''Ruins of Kumonchoja residence''' It is said that the hill on which this shrine is located was once the residence of a famous person in Oshu called ``Kumonchoja'', and the following legend remains. :Among the servants of the ruler is the princess of Itsuki Dainagon, a {{ill|lt=nobleman|Q1196276|qid=Q1196276|1=Kuge}} of {{ill|lt=Kiokuni|Q864053|qid=Q864053|lt=Kii|1=Kii Province}}. ink|Ise Grand Shrine|Q687168|Ise} There was a woman named {{ill|lt=Akutama Hime|Q85880359|qid=Q85880359|lt=Akudama|1=Akudama}} who was kidnapped on her way home from a pilgrimage and sold as a servant to a wealthy man. She prayed to her guardian deity, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Avalokitesvara|1=UNKNOWN}}, to make her appear as an ugly woman to ordinary people, but to people of high status as the beautiful princess she was. , usually had a very ugly appearance, but on the occasion of the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ezo Conquest|1=UNKNOWN}}, he stopped by the house of a wealthy man.{{ill|lt=Sakanoue Tamuramaru|Q85878315|qid=Q85878315|lt=Sakanoue Tamuramaro|1=Sakanoue Tamuramaro}}, she gave birth to a son on August 1, {{ill|lt=Enryaku|Q1373176|qid=Q1373176|1=Enryaku}}18 ({{ill|lt=799|Q33488|qid=Q33488|1=799}}), and gave birth to this child as Chikuma. He was named Senkumamaru, and when Chikumamaru turned 13, they went to Tokyo together and met Tamuramaro, and Chikumamaru became the second Tamuramaro, according to a local information board. (The signboard near the center of the image of ``Kumonchoja Residence Ruins.''). *'''Keyaki old stock''':Beside the Iidoi Inari Myojin Shrine, which is a shrine outside the precincts, under a rain cover, there is an old {{ill|lt=keyaki|Q920344|qid=Q920344|lt=keyaki|1=Zelkova serrata}} (keyaki) with a trunk circumference of about 5 to 6 meters and a black burn mark. "Rifu Village Journal" states that ``There is a large zelkova tree that is over 1,000 years old, and one tree still exists,'' and "Miyagi Prefectural History" includes a photo from 1957 ({{ill|lt=1957|Q5311|qid=Q5311|1=1957}}). “Engishiki Nairiku Oku 100 Za Heisei Pilgrimage Record”
